Just now

Photo by Kelly Sikkema on UnsplashYour carrier sold your location data. The Supreme Court just made sure that they won’t go unpunished.
Most people never read the fine print when they sign up for a phone plan. They trust their carrier. They assume the company holding their account isn’t quietly selling details about their whereabouts to strangers. Turns out that trust was misplaced for millions of Americans. And it took a Supreme Court ruling to bring any real accountability to the table.
What actually happened, in plain English?
In June 2026, the U.S. Supreme Court sided with the Federal Communications Commission in an 8–1 ruling against AT&T and Verizon. The case centered on a simple but alarming fact: major wireless carriers had sold access to customer location data to third parties without securing users’ consent.
The FCC didn’t sit on its hands. The agency imposed nearly $200 million in fines across the major carriers. T-Mobile was fined $80 million, Sprint $12 million, AT&T $57 million, and Verizon nearly $47 million.
But here’s where it gets interesting. The carriers didn’t just pay up and move on. They pushed back hard, arguing that the FCC had no constitutional right to levy fines through its own internal process. They wanted a jury trial. They said the whole system was unconstitutional.
The Supreme Court disagreed. By a landslide.
Why did the carriers fight so hard?
This wasn’t just about money. For companies the size of AT&T and Verizon, $57 million or $47 million is a rounding error in their quarterly reports. The real game was about power.
The carriers accused the FCC of using in-house proceedings to impose penalties, which they argued deprived them of their right to a jury trial under the U.S. Constitution. If they had won, it would have significantly weakened the FCC’s ability to discipline carriers quickly and effectively in the future.
Think about what that would mean for you as a consumer. Every time a carrier crosses a line, the FCC would have to go through a full court process, likely taking years, before any accountability kicks in. Fines would become theoretical. Enforcement would become sluggish. And carriers would know it.
The Court ultimately ruled that the FCC retains authority to use its informal enforcement process, and that process survives constitutional challenge. That is a meaningful win for consumer protection, even if the legal nuances are a bit dry.
What this means for everyday phone users
Here’s the part that actually matters to you.
Your location data is sensitive. It can reveal where you live, where you work, where you worship, and who you spend time with. Selling that without your knowledge is not a minor privacy hiccup. It’s a fundamental breach of trust.
The fact that the FCC has now been backed by the nation’s highest court in its ability to fine carriers for this kind of behavior sends a clear message. Carriers cannot assume they can monetize your data and face zero consequences. The regulatory teeth are real.
But here’s the uncomfortable truth: this ruling doesn’t mean it won’t happen again. It means carriers know the FCC can hold them accountable faster. That is progress, but it’s not the same as a guarantee.
This is exactly why more people, especially students, budget-conscious users, and newcomers to the US, are rethinking which carriers they actually want to trust with their information.
[Related Read: Why International Students Overpay for Mobile Service]
What transparency actually looks like
When you sign up for a mobile plan, you’re entering a relationship. You hand over your name, your payment details, and your usage data. In return, you expect service and privacy.
Smaller, more transparent carriers like Infimobile operate on a fundamentally different model. There’s no massive corporate infrastructure built around harvesting and monetizing customer data. The focus is on providing affordable, straightforward mobile service, especially for people who can’t afford to get burned by hidden terms or surprise bills.
You won’t find Infimobile facing a $57 million fine for selling your location to a data broker. That’s not a coincidence. It’s a reflection of how the business is set up.
The coverage question everyone asks.
One thing that comes up constantly when people consider switching away from the big carriers is coverage. It’s a fair concern. AT&T and Verizon have spent decades and billions of dollars building out their networks.
But here’s what’s worth knowing: most smaller carriers that offer budget-friendly plans actually operate on the same towers as the big ones. They’re called MVNOs, or mobile virtual network operators. They lease access to the major networks at wholesale rates and pass the savings on to you.
So, the idea that you have to pay Verizon or AT&T prices to get Verizon or AT&T coverage? That’s simply not true anymore. You can get reliable service across most of the US without handing your money or your location data directly to a company that just lost a Supreme Court case over data misuse.
What the ruling leaves unanswered
The Supreme Court’s decision was a win, but it wasn’t a tidy ending.
The Court left open whether AT&T, Verizon, or other carriers that paid FCC forfeitures under the pre-2026 understanding might have a valid claim for a refund. It also raised questions about the practical enforceability of FCC orders going forward, with some concern that if the Department of Justice declines to pursue collection in court, the deterrent effect of these fines could be weakened.
The majority’s reasoning that FCC orders are non-binding notices could, as Justice Thomas warned in his dissent, undermine the deterrent effect of FCC enforcement if carriers understand that non-payment triggers no immediate adverse consequences.
In other words, the system works better than it did before this ruling. But it is not airtight. And the best protection you have as a consumer is choosing who you do business with in the first place.
[Related Read: How to Choose the Right SIM Plan for College]
What students and budget users should take from this
If you’re a student, an international newcomer, or just someone trying to keep their phone bill under control, this case is more relevant to you than it might seem.
The carriers that got fined are the same ones selling premium-priced unlimited plans with multi-year contracts. They’ve built a business model around locking you in and extracting as much revenue as possible, including, apparently, from your location data.
You have real alternatives. Plans designed for people who want simple, affordable mobile service without the corporate baggage. Plans where you’re not a data point to be monetized.
A few things worth keeping in mind when comparing your options:

• Does the carrier operate on a major US network?

• Are there hidden fees buried in the billing?

• What does the carrier’s privacy policy actually say about your data?

• Is the plan flexible, or does it lock you into a long-term contract?
These aren’t complicated questions. But most people never ask them.
A simple summary before you go
The Supreme Court backed the FCC. AT&T and Verizon lost. The carriers that sold your location data without consent faced real financial consequences, and the court just confirmed the government has the authority to make that happen again if needed.
It’s a meaningful step forward for consumer protection in telecom. But your best defense is still choosing a carrier that treats your data and your wallet with respect. That’s where options like Infimobile come in, offering a path to reliable, affordable mobile service without the baggage that comes from years of big-carrier behavior.
Want to see how your current phone plan stacks up against more affordable, privacy-conscious alternatives? It’s worth taking a few minutes to compare what’s out there. You might be surprised how much you could save, and how much peace of mind you could gain.
